perturbation.original_text: Except as described elsewhere in this Agreement, the Recipient and those of its employees, officers, agents and affiliates permitted access hereunder will hold the Confidential Information in confidence and will take all necessary steps to pre- serve the confidential and proprietary nature of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as described elsewhere in this Agreement, the Recipient and those of its employees, officers, agents and affiliates permitted access hereunder will endeavor to hold the Confidential Information in confidence and will attempt to preserve the confidential and proprietary nature of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will hold' to 'will endeavor to hold' and 'will take all necessary steps' to 'will attempt to preserve' weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, creating an ambiguity as to the required level of effort. 'Endeavor' and 'attempt' are vague terms that do not guarantee actual confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the California Trade Secrets Act, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The original text was more aligned with this standard, whereas the changed text implies less stringent requirements, potentially falling short of 'reasonable efforts'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, potentially contradicting the requirement for 'reasonable efforts' to protect trade secrets, as suggested by the Law Explanation. The original text uses stronger language ('will hold,' 'will take all necessary steps'), while the changed text uses weaker, more ambiguous language ('will endeavor to hold,' 'will attempt to preserve'). Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' in California Code, CIV 3426.1, including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy,' suggesting a legal obligation to maintain secrecy. The change from 'will' to 'endeavor' and 'attempt' introduces ambiguity that could be interpreted as not fulfilling this duty, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Without limiting the foregoing, the Recipient (1) will not disclose any Confidential Information to persons within its organization who do not have a need to know in order for the Recipient to engage in the Purpose; (2) will not disclose any Confidential Information to any person outside of its organization unless such person has a need to know in order for the Recipient to engage in the Purpose, and such person is bound by fiduci-ary or contractual duties of confidentiality to the Recipient that are at least as stringent as those contained in this Agreement; (3) will, if reasonably required, cause each of the Recipient’s employees, officers, agents, affiliates and attorneys who have access to the Confidential Information to sign a confidentiality and non-disclosure agreement, for the benefit of the Discloser, containing terms at least as stringent as those contained in this Agreement, and, in any event, advise all of its employees, officers, agents, and affiliates of the confidential and proprietary nature of the Confidential Information; and (4) in all instances, exercise at least the same degree of care to maintain the secrecy of the Con- fidential Information as the Recipient exercises to protect its own most valuable trade secrets, but in no case less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Without limiting the foregoing, the Recipient (1) will consider not disclosing any Confidential Information to persons within its organization who do not have a need to know in order for the Recipient to engage in the Purpose; (2) will consider not disclosing any Confidential Information to any person outside of its organization unless such person has a need to know in order for the Recipient to engage in the Purpose, and such person is bound by fiduci-ary or contractual duties of confidentiality to the Recipient that are at least as stringent as those contained in this Agreement; (3) will, if deemed necessary, cause each of the Recipient’s employees, officers, agents, affiliates and attorneys who have access to the Confidential Information to sign a confidentiality and non-disclosure agreement, for the benefit of the Discloser, containing terms at least as stringent as those contained in this Agreement, and, in any event, advise all of its employees, officers, agents, and affiliates of the confidential and proprietary nature of the Confidential Information; and (4) in all instances, exercise the same degree of care to maintain the secrecy of the Confidential Information as the Recipient exercises to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will not disclose' to 'will consider not disclosing' weakens the commitment to non-disclosure. Additionally, changing 'if reasonably required' to 'if deemed necessary' provides more discretion to the recipient, and removing 'but in no case less than reasonable care' removes a protection for the Discloser. This introduces ambiguities regarding the specific steps the Recipient must take to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: By using words like 'consider' and 'deemed necessary', and by removing the clause that mandates at least 'reasonable care', the altered section introduces ambiguity. It may imply a standard of care lower than the 'reasonable efforts' required to protect a trade secret under California law, thus contradicting Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting the requirement to maintain secrecy under trade secret laws. The original text mandated specific actions to safeguard confidential information and guaranteed at least 'reasonable care'. The changed text weakens these obligations by using discretionary language like 'consider' and 'deemed necessary,' and eliminates the 'but in no case less than reasonable care' clause. According to the provided information, this introduces ambiguity and suggests a potentially lower standard of care. Although the scraped snippet doesn't explicitly mention 'reasonable efforts', it discusses the breach of a duty to maintain secrecy which is weakened here. This falls under Ambiguity Legal Contradiction because the ambiguity introduced by weakening the commitment makes the obligation harder to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees to protect Confidential Information provided to it by the Discloser with at least the same degree of care as it protects its own confidential, proprietary, and/or trade secret information and shall promptly advise the Discloser, in writing, of any misappropriation or misuse, of which it becomes aware, of Confidential Information by any person.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will endeavor to protect Confidential Information provided to it by the Discloser with the same degree of care it deems necessary and shall advise the Discloser of any suspected misappropriation or misuse of Confidential Information by any person, if feasible.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to protect' to 'will endeavor to protect' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'at least the same degree of care as it protects its own' with 'the same degree of care it deems necessary' allows the recipient to subjectively determine the level of protection. The addition of 'suspected' before 'misappropriation' introduces a higher threshold for reporting and the added 'if feasible' provides an escape clause, making this obligation ambiguous and less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the protection of confidential information subject to the Recipient's own judgement of 'necessary' care and adding the 'if feasible' clause, this modification introduces ambiguity and lowers the standard of care. This contradicts Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1, which requires a higher standard of reasonable measures, meaning that 'if feasible' does not always satisfy the degree of care required under law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity and weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially contradicting legal standards for trade secret protection. The original text mandates protection 'with at least the same degree of care' as the party's own confidential information. The changed text replaces this with 'the same degree of care it deems necessary' and adds 'if feasible'. Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1 defines 'misappropriation' and implies a duty to maintain secrecy. By allowing the receiving party to subjectively determine the level of care and adding an 'if feasible' escape clause, the modified text undermines the duty to maintain secrecy. This subjective determination and the feasibility clause could allow a party to provide a level of care that is less than reasonable under the circumstances, potentially leading to misappropriation of trade secrets as defined by the statute. This contradicts the implicit requirement for reasonable measures to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the written request of the Discloser, the Recipient shall either destroy or return to the Discloser any and all Confidential Information in the Recipient’s possession, except for one copy, which copy may be retained for archival purposes in a secure file.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the written request of the Discloser, the Recipient shall consider destroying or returning to the Discloser Confidential Information in the Recipient’s possession. The Recipient can retain a copy for archival purposes.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall either destroy or return' to 'shall consider destroying or returning' makes the action optional. Removing 'any and all' limits the scope of what needs to be returned or destroyed. Removing 'except for one copy, which copy may be retained for archival purposes in a secure file' and replacing with 'The Recipient can retain a copy for archival purposes' removes the 'secure file' requirement and implies they can retain any number of copies.
perturbation.law_explanation: The new modifications make the return or destruction of confidential information uncertain, undermining the 'reasonable efforts' required under the California Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1). The original text explicitly required a return or destruction of the files, and a secure archival of one copy, whereas the changed text is ambiguous and provides avenues for exploitation.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding the return or destruction of confidential information, which contradicts the requirement for reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets. The original text mandated the return or destruction of 'any and all' confidential information, whereas the changed text uses 'shall consider,' making the action optional. Additionally, it removes the 'secure file' requirement for the archival copy. This shift undermines the obligation to maintain secrecy as outlined in California Civil Code § 3426.1, which defines 'misappropriation' to include the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent. By making the return or destruction of confidential information uncertain and relaxing the secure archival requirements, the changed text creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Neither Party shall transfer or assign any rights or delegate any ob- ligations herein, in whole or in part, whether voluntarily or by operation of law, without the prior written consent of the other Party, which shall not be unreasonably withheld.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither Party shall endeavor to transfer or assign any rights or delegate any ob- ligations herein, in whole or in part, whether voluntarily or by operation of law, without the prior written consent of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall not transfer' to 'shall endeavor to transfer', the action is no longer prohibited, just discouraged. Also, removing 'which shall not be unreasonably withheld' allows the other party to deny consent for any reason, or no reason at all.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While this section is typically applied to non-compete agreements, the ambiguous language in the modified clause, particularly removing the 'which shall not be unreasonably withheld' part, grants a party excessive power to block assignments, potentially hindering a party's ability to conduct business, which can then be used to say they are restricted from certain business activities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not transfer' to 'shall endeavor to transfer' weakens the prohibition of transferring rights, and removing 'which shall not be unreasonably withheld' grants the other party unchecked power to block assignments. According to BPC 16600(a), 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By allowing a party to unreasonably withhold consent for assignment, the modified contract creates a scenario where a party might be unduly restricted from conducting business, thereby contradicting BPC 16600(a).
